texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane contactors are considered as one of the most promising strategies for intensified gas-liquid absorption processes. The key concept of a membrane contactor is to make use of a porous, hydrophobic membrane material that separates the gas and the liquid streams in a gas absorption process. A supplementary mass transfer resistance (due to the membrane) obviously needs to be considered, especially when compared to a classical gas-liquid absorption situation where there is direct contact between the two phases. Nevertheless, the possibility to develop an extremely high interfacial area (a) thanks to the membrane fibers, as opposed to classical gas-liquid equipments, is expected to significantly counterbalance the decrease of the overall mass transfer coefficient ( K OV). As a consequence, a much higher effective mass transfer performance (that primarily depends on the product K OV · a ) can possibly be obtained, leading to an intensification effect. Impressive reduction factors of the size of the equipment, with figures ranging from 10 [1] up to 30 [2] for acid gas scrubbing, have been mentioned.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The use of membrane contactors for post-combustion CO2 capture by absorption into a chemical solvent is currently one of the most intensively investigated topics in membrane science. The main (if not the only) target of employing membrane contactors for this application is to provide a significant reduction in size of the absorption unit, as compared to the classical gas-liquid absorption technology, based on packed columns. Surprisingly, this key performance characteristic, which is best expressed through a so-called intensification factor, remains essentially unreported in studies dedicated to CO2 absorption in membrane contactors. In this article, we recall the state of the art in terms of operating conditions and process performance for packed columns applied to CO2 capture. Based on this, a series of challenges for membrane material design and critical process engineering issues are discussed in order to set up a methodology for a better comparison between membrane contactors and packed columns. More specifically, an average volumetric CO2 absorption capacity around 1 mol CO2 m -3 s -1 is proposed as a baseline performance of packed columns in order to estimate the intensification factor of membrane contactors. © 2012 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

texts.orig: For post-combustion CO2 capture, the achievement of a major reduction in size of installation is indeed of primary importance in order to possibly decrease the capital cost of the absorption unit. Consequently, a rigorous evaluation of the intensification factor (namely the volume reduction ratio of the gas absorption unit obtained by the membrane contactor process) should logically be systematically considered. This target is obviously best achieved through a comparison of the membrane contactor performance with the absorption capacity of classical gas-liquid operations performed in packed columns. Surprisingly, we notice that, apart from in very few publications, such a comparison has rarely been reported. Consequently, even though intensification remains the major (if not the only) interest of membrane contactors for CO2 capture, this key parameter is essentially an unexplored issue.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The present article first recalls the framework of CO2 absorption in packed columns for post-combustion capture. This has been taken as a baseline case. We then provide a critical analysis of the major differences between the studies performed for CO2 post combustion capture by absorption columns and compare them to a membrane contactor framework. Based on this, we discuss the major challenge when it comes to materials, and propose a more relevant methodology in order to perform a rigorous comparison between the two types of technologies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Absorption in a chemical solvent is a mature technology, especially for acid gas removal. Moreover, this technology is unambiguously considered as the reference for first generation processes for CO2 capture in post-combustion [3]. A very large number of solvents have been tested in order to optimize the selectivity, capacity and regeneration energy trade-offs for flue gas treatment, but monoethanolamine (MEA), usually in a 30 wt% aqueous solution, is most often taken as the reference solvent for post-combustion carbon capture processes.

texts.orig: Coming back to the potential place and role of membrane contactors for this application, no significant improvement in the specific energy requirement E can be expected. This parameter is principally governed by the nature of the solvent, which remains mostly unchanged when an absorption column is replaced by a membrane contactor. Moreover, research has been limited almost exclusively to the absorption section unit for membrane contactor applications because of the temperature constraints in the stripper unit. The potential of membrane contactors for stripping operations, thanks to temperature-resistant polymers or microporous inorganic membranes, should be more systematically investigated. This key issue has been addressed very recently [4,5,20].

texts.orig: Various advantages of membrane contactors can occasionally be proposed, for instance as a means of avoiding a tower (in typhoon areas) or motion resistance (for off-shore applications). Nevertheless, as explained in the introduction, the most important role that can be played by a membrane contactor in post combustion carbon capture is related to the decrease of the size of the absorption unit. In that respect, an average figure of 1 mol m -3 s -1 for the volumetric CO2 absorption capacity could be taken as a baseline case for packed columns.

texts.orig: 3. Membrane contactors for CO2 capture: intensification factor
texts.text: 3. Membrane contactors for CO2 capture: intensification factor

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To the best of our knowledge, the exact evaluation of the intensification factor of membrane contactors has rarely been addressed in the numerous publications dedicated to CO2 capture applications (Fig. 1). Several results for membrane contactors are nevertheless available for CO2/natural gas applications at a small demonstration stage [17,18]. However, for post-combustion flue gas treatment, very few studies propose a direct comparison, through experimental data, of packed columns and membrane contactors under similar operating conditions. A tentative inventory of publications regarding a comparison between packed columns and membrane contactors based on experimental results is summarized in Table 2.

texts.orig: More generally, the critical membrane contactor characteristics, i.e., material (gas permeance), hollow fiber (inner diameter and thickness) and module (packing factor, length), which enable a significant intensification factor to be achieved, are not available. Contrary to other engineering applications, there are no master curves that may propose an estimate of the intensification potential from a set of membrane material or module data. As a consequence, the exact potential of a new material or module, only occasionally reported in journals, can hardly be directly estimated, unless the overall volumetric absorption capacity is clearly specified.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Material selection
texts.text: 4.1. Material selection

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Selecting the right polymeric material for membrane contactor applications is logically of major importance. For CO2 capture applications, a great number of materials and structures have been investigated. It is however important to stress that long-term contact with solvent solutions can lead to significant degradation. These material compatibility issues have been occasionally reported, but are not always taken into account. For instance, amine aqueous solutions (such as MEA MonoEthanolAmine), which is the reference solvent for this application, have been shown to induce irreversible degradation reactions in contact with polyethylene (PE), polypropylene (PP) or polyvinylidenefluoride (PVDF) membrane materials.
texts.text: Selecting the right polymeric material for membrane contactor applications is logically of major importance. For CO2 capture applications, a great number of materials and structures have been investigated. It is however important to stress that long-term contact with solvent solutions can lead to significant degradation. These material compatibility issues have been occasionally reported, but are not always taken into account. For instance, amine aqueous solutions (such as MEA MonoEthanolAmine), which is the reference solvent for this application, have been shown to induce irreversible degradation reactions in contact with polyethylene (PE), polypropylene (PP) or polyvinylidenefluoride (PVDF) membrane materials.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In these cases, the chemical reactivity of amine solvents can result in various changes of the membrane properties, such as the pore structure [21], interfacial characteristics (with a tendency to promote wetting effects [22]), gas permeability [23] or gas-liquid mass transfer efficiency [24,25]. The limited stability of PVDF, PE, PP membranes to amine solvents on long timescales addresses major issues with regard to the applicability of these materials for membrane contactor applications at the industrial scale. It is striking that numerous studies involve amine solvents for these materials, with experiments most often performed on timescales of an hour or a day. A more realistic approach would be to use, almost exclusively, fluorinated polymers such as polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) together with amine solvents, in order to ensure chemical resistance, mechanical stability and a very high hydrophobicity. Alternatively, PP, PE and PVDF membranes can possibly withstand long contact times with less aggressive CO2 capture solvents such as amino acid salt solutions (Fig. 3).

texts.orig: 4.2. Resistance to wetting
texts.text: 4.2. Resistance to wetting

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Apart from the material/solvent compatibility issues, the gradual wetting of the membrane by the liquid solvent is also a major problem for long-term use of membrane contactors. In some cases, this unwanted phenomenon can directly result from morphological changes of the pore structure and/or size distribution, such as those related to chemical and/or physical effects induced by the solvent, as mentioned above.
texts.text: Apart from the material/solvent compatibility issues, the gradual wetting of the membrane by the liquid solvent is also a major problem for long-term use of membrane contactors. In some cases, this unwanted phenomenon can directly result from morphological changes of the pore structure and/or size distribution, such as those related to chemical and/or physical effects induced by the solvent, as mentioned above.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nevertheless, even when a structurally stable and chemically compatible membrane material is used, wetting can occur. According to the equation that governs the liquid entry pressure conditions, a decrease of the solvent's interfacial tension or a pressure head shock on the fluid system can promote a limited or global wetting of the porous structure. Protection strategies have been proposed in order to possibly limit unwanted pressure shock effects for industrial membrane contactor installations [26]. However, from a practical point of view, wetting appears to be extremely difficult to prevent and control on long timescales [28].

texts.orig: Taking a simple resistance in series approach, a relationship between the effective membrane mass transfer coefficient ( k m) and the wetting ratio ( /DC2 ) can be proposed. For a flat membrane of thickness z and porosity ε , the gas phase and the liquid phase thickness will be (1 -/DC2 ) · z and /DC2 · z , respectively, for non tortuous pore geometry. The mass transfer resistance can then be calculated from the diffusion coefficient in the gas and liquid phases D G and D L , the liquid/gas equilibrium solubility coefficient H and, if a chemical reaction takes place in the liquid phase, the enhancement factor E . For a flat membrane of thickness z and porosity ε , the gas phase and the liquid phase thickness will be (1 -/DC2 ) · z and /DC2 · z , respectively, for non tortuous pore geometry. The mass transfer resistance can then be calculated from the diffusion coefficient in the gas and liquid phases D G and D L , the liquid/gas equilibrium solubility coefficient H and, if a chemical reaction takes place in the liquid phase, the enhancement factor E . Assuming straight pores (i.e. a tortuosity factor of 1), the final expression of the effective membrane mass transfer coefficient is

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: k m = ε z ( D G · D L (1 -/DC2 ) · D L + ( /DC2 · D G /H · E ) ) (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 4 shows that, even for a very small wetting ratio /DC2 , a large decrease of the membrane mass transfer coefficient can be obtained. This high sensitivity directly affects the gas-liquid mass transfer performances. The decrease is more important when the liquid phase resistance is maximized. This corresponds to a situation where the gases are poorly soluble in the liquid (i.e. for low H · E values). In any case, Fig. 4 shows that even a very small wetting of the membrane material can potentially drastically decrease the absorption performances of a membrane contactor. Taking into account the CO2/MEA system, the H value is close to 1, while E typically ranges between 100 and 300 in the absorption unit. It is important to notice that, even under these relatively favorable conditions, a decrease of the membrane mass transfer coefficient of almost 1 order of magnitude can occur for only 10% of wetting.

texts.orig: In such a context, the potentialities offered by dense skin composite membrane materials are particularly attractive. Interestingly, dense self-standing polymeric films were initially proposed in the 60s for gas-liquid absorption for water treatment applications [40] before being replaced by more permeable hydrophobic microporous membranes. The concept has later been re-examined with dense skin composite membranes for gas absorption and vapor separation applications [41-43]. For the specific case of amine solvents, a thin dense skin of a highly permeable, chemically resistant polymer such as Teflon-AF appears to be particularly promising. The mass transfer performances in such a case can indeed comparable to nonwetted microporous membranes [44] and can remain stable over time [45]. The dense skin concept could be an interesting solution for CO2 capture, as it provides longterm resistance to wetting and possibly predictable mass transfer coefficients. However, the possible problem of capillary condensation should be considered carefully when designing the support. It is interesting to notice that this solution has been adopted for membrane oxygenators for medical applications [46].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5.2. Modeling and scale-up issues
texts.text: 5.2. Modeling and scale-up issues

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, given the impressive number of publications that tackle the modeling and simulation challenges of membrane contactors for CO2 capture, it might be expected that rigorous scale-up strategies are available, so that errors associated when assuming constant K OV · a , such as those shown in Fig. 5, are circumvented. We would finally like to address a series of critical issues in terms of scaleup strategies for membrane contactors, which, to a large extent, have rarely been taken into account until now. In the large majority of publications on membrane contactors for CO2 absorption in a chemical solvent, 2D models are classically proposed, thanks to the impressive computing facilities that are available nowadays for chemical engineers. Laminar flow is most often assumed inside the fibers (parabolic profile) whereas there is a plug flow outside. Chemical kinetics (in some cases very sophisticated) are described thanks to the large amount of data available for the CO2-MEA system. In the vast majority of publications, there is a very good (if not excellent) agreement between simulations and experimental results, almost invariably obtained in lab-scale contactors (typically 0.1-0.3 m length for 0.01-0.05 m diameter).

Laminar flow is most often assumed inside the fibers (parabolic profile) whereas there is a plug flow outside. Chemical kinetics (in some cases very sophisticated) are described thanks to the large amount of data available for the CO2-MEA system. In the vast majority of publications, there is a very good (if not excellent) agreement between simulations and experimental results, almost invariably obtained in lab-scale contactors (typically 0.1-0.3 m length for 0.01-0.05 m diameter).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nevertheless, we consider that several important issues have to be rigorously addressed, for scale-up studies:
texts.text: Nevertheless, we consider that several important issues have to be rigorously addressed, for scale-up studies:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -Pressure drop effects, especially on the gas side , are of major interest but are almost systematically neglected. Coming back to Table 1, an industrial-scale membrane contactor has to maintain a pressure drop on the gas side that is low enough to be compatible with energy requirement constraints. Large-scale membrane contactors usually require high packing fractions (in order to maximize the intensification factor) and much larger fiber lengths than lab-scale units. This can result in unacceptable pressure drop levels, far above the 7000 Pa limit indicated in Table 1 -a point that should be carefully taken into account. More concretely, a 20 000 Pa pressure drop on the gas phase corresponds to 50-60 MW extra power loss in a 500 MW power plant [47]. The possible increase in energy requirement induced by a too intensified membrane contactor must thus be clearly estimated.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -Temperature effects are also known to significantly influence the absorption performance. In the large majority of membrane contactor simulation studies, isothermal conditions are assumed, for the sake of simplicity. This hypothesis does not even hold for lab-scale experiments. Moreover, at a larger scale, nonisothermal conditions will be basic operating conditions. To the best of our knowledge, this point has only been taken into account by very few studies and corresponds to a complex situation, in link with the next item.

This perturbation can significantly decrease the performances of a large-scale membrane contactor and efficient module design will be needed in order to minimize the penalty. Several factors such as a variation in fiber diameter for lumen side flow, irregular bundle distribution for shell side flow, wall effects . . . can generate flow disturbances and significant departures from ideal flow. This topic is again essentially unexplored. More importantly, we are not aware of a single publication that addresses the design and hydrodynamic dispersion characteristics on large-scale membrane contactors.
